channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: TCL Shows Off New Gaming Monitors With Up To 320 Hz And Stunning HDR
item.content:encoded:
Summer Game Fest is a place for people to check out upcoming video games, but an important part of playing video games is what you're playing them on. I had a chance to be walked through three new Gaming Monitors from TCL, where they've taken the QD-Mini LED technology from their TVs and scaled it down to be a gaming monitor. Obviously, this is very hard to demonstrate remotely, so I was excited to see just how these monitors performed in person.

I was walked through the new technology in the C2A Pro, C2A, and P3A in a series of demos showing off the vibrancy of color in Astro's PlayRoom running on PS5, the HDR benefits in The Last of Us: Part II running on PS5, and finally with Call of Duty on a PC.

Before getting into the examples that they showed me, I'm going to go through the specs of each of the devices.

 

C1A Pro

C2A

P3A

Screen Size27"27"27"
Panel Resolution3840 × 21603840 × 21602560 × 1440
Refresh Rate4K@160Hz / FHD@320Hz4K@160Hz / FHD@320Hz320 Hz
Local Dimming Zones2,30411961196
HDRDisplay HDR1400Display HDR1000Display HDR1000
Brightness (Peak)2,000 Nits1,200 Nits1,200 Nits
Color Depth and Accuracy1.07 billion / 8bit+2bitFRC
ΔE < 2		1.07 billion / 8bit+2bitFRC
ΔE < 2		1.07 billion / 8bit+2bitFRC
I/O
  • 2x HDMI 2.1
  • 2x DP 1.4
  • 1x USB-C
  • 2x USB-A
  • 1x USB-B
  • 1x 3.5mm Audio Out
  • 2x HDMI 2.1
  • 1x DP 1.4
  • 1x 3.5mm Audio Out
  • 2x HDMI 2.1
  • 1x DP 1.4
  • 1x 3.5mm Audio Out

Starting on the C2A Pro and showing off Astro's Playroom, I was walked through the basics of the technology that they had put into this new device. "You've got 2,300 local dimming zones and 2000 nits in terms of peak brightness. You have color uniformity and accuracy of 98% GCI."

I was also informed that, for color, these monitors are pre-calibrated, so what you receive is a monitor that you shouldn't need to do any additional tweaking once it's in your home or office.

Looking at Astro's Playroom, the level Bot Beach was loaded up. The first view of this stage is a blue fan spinning above crystal clear blue water and blue colored ice forming a ring around the stage. Altering the settings on the PlayStation 5, HDR was turned on, and returning to the level, the color vibrancy was bumped up to 11. Here, the fan at the center of the level almost turned a light purple, while the ice and water had incredibly distinct shades of blue.

This color accuracy isn't just important in the gaming space, but for creatives who frequently rely on creating something color accurate in video creation, graphic work, or advertising, so that you can be confident that how it shows on this display will be how it shows best. Obviously, working to perfectly color grade a video on this monitor will lose some of its flair if the next person to watch it is on a decade old monitor, but it will at least let you get accuracy for everyone.

The monitors all have TCL Motion Clarity technology that functions in two different modes. TCL Motion Clarity, as the name would suggest, means that even when things are moving quickly, with a response time of 0.2 milliseconds, whether you're running the monitor in 1080p at 320 Hz or 4k at 160 Hz, you'll get a clear image. "Usually, an IPS panel or a VA panel, there's one millisecond gray to gray. Ours is 0.2 milliseconds."

Explaining how this was possible, he compared the traditional way that a screen refreshes by going entirely black and then on again. The whole image is refreshed at a time faster than your eye can perceive; this is why, if you're recording a screen with a fast enough camera, you'll see it flickering. TCL instead performs its scanning line by line. I joked that it was almost similar to the way that CRT monitors would refresh themselves, and I was told it's a very similar idea.

Another side effect of the Motion Clarity process is that because the entire display isn't turning off briefly to refresh, more of the image remains bright for longer. This means that the 2000 nits of peak brightness is going to be more consistently what you're seeing.

Moving on to The Last of Us Part II to show off more of the HDR and brightness appointments. We loaded into the game as Ellie, standing outside the store filled with infected. This was a strangely beautiful place to show off the range of on-screen color between the pure white show and cloudy skies, and the slaughtered moose with pools of dark blood collecting around it.

With HDR off, there was a lot of blurring between the whites and greys of the scene, leaving it looking like a far cloudier day. When HDR was switched on, the sun was shining, the light wasn't anywhere near as flat, and as the camera panned up into the sky, there was an incredibly sharp lens flare.

Moving away from the brights, the color gradient between pristine snow, grey snow, bloody snow, and then the deep pool of dark red blood.

My guide joked that he wished there was a faster way for the PlayStation 5 to shift between HDR On and Off instead of having to go through 4-5 menus. He added that this level of brightness and color clarity wouldn't be possible on OLED displays because of the lower level of nits that it reaches.

The final test that I was walked through was getting my hands on with Call of Duty running at 200 FPS. This was a good test of the low latency and color accuracy again, but unfortunately, due to the questionable internet at the venue, it made showing off the quick refresh a bit jarring when I was lag switching all over the map.

Instead, the monitor got swapped over to the browser and into the 1080p/320Hz mode, and Blur Buster was pulled up. I was shown the clarity of a UFO moving across the screen at 320Hz, and the image remained perfectly clear the entire time.

There was a clear impact from my time checking out these new TCL monitors for how the HDR and Color Accuracy really made every image pop. While refresh rate and low response time for gaming were demonstrated it would definitely need to be something more time is spent with to fully grasp. The C2A Pro, C2A, and P3A will all be available later this month, starting at $499.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/technology/previews/tcl-shows-off-new-gaming-monitors-with-up-to-320-hz-and-stunning-hdr
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
